objective.subject: English
objective.statement: Construct and evaluate coherent arguments using claims, reasons, relevant evidence, logical links, counterarguments and rebuttals.
misconceptions: Treats a strongly worded opinion as if it were evidence.
Lists several points without showing how they support the main claim.
Uses evidence that is related to the topic but does not prove the reason.
Assumes acknowledging a counterargument automatically weakens the writer's position.
objective.parent_explanation: Can organise a reasoned argument, choose relevant evidence and respond fairly to an opposing point.
objective.teacher_evidence: Maps claims, reasons and evidence accurately; writes coherent supported paragraphs; and distinguishes rebuttal from dismissal or topic change.

activities.title: I can build a clear argument and support each reason with relevant evidence.
activities.prompt: Build the argument route: claim, reason, evidence, counterargument, rebuttal.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Sort each sentence as a topic, an opinion or an arguable claim.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Reactivate sentence purpose and distinguish a debatable claim from a broad topic or unsupported preference.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A useful claim takes a clear position that reasons and evidence can support.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Sentence cards move into topic, opinion and arguable-claim columns.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Build the argument route: claim, reason, evidence, counterargument, rebuttal.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce argument as connected reasoning rather than a collection of persuasive phrases.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A strong argument has a route. Each reason supports the claim, and each piece of evidence supports a reason.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A clean argument map grows from a central claim into supported branches.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Why is the survey result evidence for extending library opening hours?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model the difference between a reason, evidence and an explanation of relevance.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The survey matters because it shows that many pupils need access after the current closing time.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A claim card connects to a reason, a survey statistic and a relevance sentence.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Rank the evidence from strongest to weakest for this reason, then explain your top choice.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Practise selecting evidence for relevance, sufficiency and credibility rather than vividness alone.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Ask whether the evidence directly supports this reason and whether the reader can trust it.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Evidence cards sit on a relevance-and-reliability scale.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Choose the rebuttal that answers the counterargument fairly without changing the subject.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check whether learners can respond to an opposing point with precise reasoning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A rebuttal should answer the real challenge, not dismiss the person making it.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Three rebuttal bridges are tested against one counterargument.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Nova why three facts do not make an argument unless their relevance is explained.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Consolidate the relationship between evidence and reasoning through verbal explanation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: I found three facts about the topic. What must I add so the reader can follow my reasoning?
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion holds disconnected fact cards beside a completed argument route.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The main position that an argument asks the reader to accept.
vocabulary.audio_script: A claim is the main position your argument will support.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A central statement card at the top of an argument map.

vocabulary.child_explanation: Information, examples or facts used to support a reason.
vocabulary.audio_script: Evidence gives the reader a sound basis for accepting a reason.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Evidence cards locking underneath a reason.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A reasonable point that challenges the writer's claim.
vocabulary.audio_script: A counterargument shows what a thoughtful person on the other side might say.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A contrasting branch entering the argument map.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A response that answers a counterargument and refocuses the main claim.
vocabulary.audio_script: A rebuttal responds fairly, then explains why the main claim still stands.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A response bridge reconnecting the counterargument to the claim.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The quality of ideas fitting together in a clear, logical order.
vocabulary.audio_script: Coherence means the reader can follow how each part of the argument connects.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: An unbroken route through claim, reason, evidence and conclusion.

manipulative.purpose: Make the hierarchy and logical connections between claim, reasons, evidence, counterargument and rebuttal visible and editable.
manipulative.interaction_notes: Learners drag sentence cards into roles, connect evidence to the reason it supports, rank sources, reorder branches and test whether a rebuttal answers the stated challenge.
manipulative.accessibility_notes: Keyboard and switch-access movement, high-contrast role patterns, text-to-speech, dyslexia-friendly spacing and a static outline view support SEND access without relying on colour or motion.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a calm paper-and-ink board, steady focus outlines and no competitive timers or flashing verdicts.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace connecting and ranking animations with static numbered cards and persistent role labels.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read argument cards aloud by role, with replay and an option to hear the completed reasoning route.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use line focus, adjustable spacing, short card-sized chunks and icons for claim, reason, evidence and response.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Reveal one branch at a time and keep the current reasoning question visible while unused cards are dimmed.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow planning time, card rehearsal and a printable or static outline before paragraph construction.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Credit a sound reason or relevant evidence separately, then offer one precise next repair instead of rejecting the whole argument.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach claim, reason, evidence, supports, however, counterargument and rebuttal; provide spoken rehearsal and connective frames.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Distinguish claims, reasons, evidence and commentary in short age-appropriate arguments.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: original issue + six sentence cards + labelled argument roles + explanation feedback
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Issues must allow reasonable disagreement and avoid requiring specialist or current-affairs knowledge.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Judge which evidence most directly and credibly supports a particular reason.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: claim + reason + four evidence cards varying in relevance, specificity and reliability
variant_blueprints.review_notes: The strongest choice must be defensible from information supplied in the item; distractors should be plausible, not absurd.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Order and connect claim, reason, evidence and explanation into coherent paragraphs.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: scrambled paragraph units + connective bank + optional reasoning stem + multiple valid surface orders
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Markers should accept coherent alternatives and avoid enforcing one mechanical paragraph formula.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Select fair counterarguments and rebuttals that directly answer them with measured reasoning.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: claim + supported reason + counterargument + four rebuttal choices mapped to misconception routes
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid insulting or straw-person distractors; model respectful disagreement and precise qualification.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain argument-role recognition and logical-link checking across curriculum subjects.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: short history, science, school or community claim + one missing or misplaced argument component
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep retrieval texts concise and ensure disciplinary content is fully supplied within the prompt.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats a strongly worded opinion as if it were evidence.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner labels an emphatic judgement or slogan as evidence.
repair_sequence: Separate what the writer believes from what the writer can show.
Test whether the sentence supplies a fact, example, observation or source.
Replace the slogan with evidence and add a sentence explaining its relevance.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner distinguishes a claim from evidence and selects a supporting item in a new context.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Lists several points without showing how they support the main claim.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner produces a sequence of related statements with no hierarchy or reasoning links.
repair_sequence: Place the main claim at the centre of an argument map.
Attach each reason to the claim and each evidence item to one reason.
Add a because, therefore or this suggests sentence that makes the logical link explicit.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner builds a paragraph in which evidence is interpreted and connected to the claim.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Uses evidence that is related to the topic but does not prove the reason.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner chooses an interesting fact whose subject matches but whose meaning does not support the stated reason.
repair_sequence: Underline the exact idea in the reason that needs support.
Rank evidence by direct relevance before considering vividness.
Explain in one sentence how the strongest evidence supports the reason.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner selects and justifies directly relevant evidence for a fresh reason.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Assumes acknowledging a counterargument automatically weakens the writer's position.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner omits reasonable challenges or dismisses them without response.
repair_sequence: Identify the strongest fair challenge to the claim.
Build a rebuttal that answers the challenge with evidence, qualification or a practical solution.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner writes a fair counterargument and a direct, measured rebuttal.

feedback.companion_prompt: Teach Nova why three facts do not make an argument unless their relevance is explained.
feedback.parent_summary: Can organise a reasoned argument, choose relevant evidence and respond fairly to an opposing point.
feedback.teacher_evidence: Maps claims, reasons and evidence accurately; writes coherent supported paragraphs; and distinguishes rebuttal from dismissal or topic change.
next_actions: Interleave argument structure with source credibility and disciplinary vocabulary.
Move from card-built paragraphs to independently planned multi-paragraph arguments.
Revisit evidence relevance when a learner relies on assertion or topic-matching facts.

body.prompt: Place each sentence in the correct role for an argument about extending the school library's opening hours.
sentences: The school library should remain open until 5 pm twice a week.
Some pupils need a quiet place to study after lessons.
In a school survey, 68% of respondents said they would use a supervised after-school study space.
However, later opening would require staff time.
A two-day trial with a rota would test demand while limiting the extra staffing needed.

value: claim: The school library should remain open until 5 pm twice a week.
reason: Some pupils need a quiet place to study after lessons.
evidence: In a school survey, 68% of respondents said they would use a supervised after-school study space.
counterargument: However, later opening would require staff time.
rebuttal: A two-day trial with a rota would test demand while limiting the extra staffing needed.

hints: The rebuttal should answer the staffing concern rather than repeat the claim.
questions.explanation: The claim states the position; the reason explains why; the survey supports that reason; the staffing point challenges the proposal; and the trial responds directly to that challenge.

body.prompt: Which evidence most strongly supports the reason that a refill station could reduce single-use plastic bottles at school?
choices: A pupil says the new station would look modern.
A one-week waste audit found 430 disposable drinks bottles in school bins.
Plastic can be made in many colours.
A poster about oceans received 25 likes.
body.reason: A refill station could reduce single-use plastic bottles at school.

expected_answer.value: A one-week waste audit found 430 disposable drinks bottles in school bins.

hints: Choose evidence about the scale of disposable-bottle use in this school.
A specific measurement is stronger here than a preference or unrelated fact.
questions.explanation: The waste audit directly measures the problem the proposal aims to reduce and gives specific school-based evidence.

body.prompt: Order the sentences into a coherent argument paragraph.
sentences: This suggests that a short movement break can improve readiness to learn rather than waste lesson time.
Year 7 classes should have a two-minute movement break during long afternoon lessons.
In a four-week class trial, the number of reminders needed in the final twenty minutes fell from an average of nine to five.
One reason is that concentration can drop when pupils sit still for an extended period.

value: Year 7 classes should have a two-minute movement break during long afternoon lessons.
One reason is that concentration can drop when pupils sit still for an extended period.
In a four-week class trial, the number of reminders needed in the final twenty minutes fell from an average of nine to five.
This suggests that a short movement break can improve readiness to learn rather than waste lesson time.

hints: Begin with the position, then give the reason.
Place the explanation after the evidence it interprets.
questions.explanation: The paragraph moves from claim to reason to evidence, then explains how the evidence supports the claim.

body.prompt: A writer argues that the school should publish a monthly pupil newspaper. Which rebuttal best answers the counterargument that printing it would waste paper?
choices: Anyone who dislikes newspapers is wrong.
The newspaper would be interesting and paper is not the main issue.
Publishing a digital edition by default, with a small number of shared printed copies, would give access while limiting paper use.
Many schools have clubs, so the newspaper should happen.

expected_answer.value: Publishing a digital edition by default, with a small number of shared printed copies, would give access while limiting paper use.

hints: The response must address paper use directly.
Look for a measured solution rather than an attack or a change of subject.
questions.explanation: The digital-first proposal accepts the legitimate concern and answers it with a practical way to reduce paper use.

reward_payload.explanation: Can organise a reasoned argument, choose relevant evidence and respond fairly to an opposing point.
reward_payload.companion_prompt: Teach Nova why three facts do not make an argument unless their relevance is explained.
